examples.title: Cloud Cost Estimator - Demonstrating Cost-Effectiveness Benefit
examples.description: A simple calculator that compares on-premise infrastructure costs vs cloud costs, demonstrating the cost-effectiveness benefit of cloud computing.
examples.code:
# Cloud Cost Estimator - Demonstrating Cost-Effectiveness Benefit
# Benefit: Pay-as-you-go model reduces upfront capital expenditure

def calculate_on_premise_cost(servers, power_per_server_kwh, hours_per_month, electricity_rate_per_kwh, maintenance_annual):
    """Calculate monthly on-premise infrastructure costs"""
    daily_power_cost = servers * power_per_server_kwh * hours_per_day * electricity_rate_per_kwh
    monthly_power = daily_power_cost * 30
    monthly_maintenance = maintenance_annual / 12
    return monthly_power + monthly_maintenance

def calculate_cloud_cost(instances, hours_per_month, hourly_rate):
    """Calculate monthly cloud infrastructure costs"""
    return instances * hours_per_month * hourly_rate

# Example comparison
servers = 5
hours_per_day = 24
hours_per_month = 730  # ~30 days
power_per_server_kwh = 0.5  # 500 watts
electricity_rate = 0.08  # per kWh
maintenance_annual = 12000  # annual maintenance

cloud_rate = 0.05  # per hour for small instance
cloud_instances = 3  # fewer needed due to auto-scaling

on_premise = calculate_on_premise_cost(servers, power_per_server_kwh, hours_per_day, electricity_rate, maintenance_annual)
cloud = calculate_cloud_cost(cloud_instances, hours_per_month, cloud_rate)

print("=== Cost Comparison: On-Premise vs Cloud ===")
print(f"On-Premise Monthly Cost: ${on_premise:.2f}")
print(f"Cloud Monthly Cost: ${cloud:.2f}")
print(f"Savings with Cloud: ${on_premise - cloud:.2f} ({(on_premise - cloud)/on_premise*100:.1f}%)")
print("\nBENEFIT DEMONSTRATED: Cloud eliminates upfront hardware costs and reduces operational overhead")
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates the cost-effectiveness benefit of cloud computing by comparing traditional on-premise infrastructure costs (including electricity, hardware, maintenance) with cloud-based costs. The pay-as-you-go model allows businesses to avoid large upfront capital investments and only pay for resources they use.

examples.title: Auto-Scaling Simulation - Demonstrating Scalability Benefit
examples.description: Simulates auto-scaling behavior in cloud environments, showing how cloud resources dynamically adjust to handle varying workloads - a key benefit of cloud computing.
examples.code:
# Auto-Scaling Simulation - Demonstrating Scalability Benefit
# Benefit: Dynamic scaling handles traffic spikes efficiently

import time
import random

class AutoScaler:
    def __init__(self, min_instances, max_instances, target_cpu_utilization):
        self.min_instances = min_instances
        self.max_instances = max_instances
        self.target_cpu = target_cpu_utilization
        self.current_instances = min_instances
    
    def get_cpu_utilization(self, request_count):
        """Simulate CPU load based on requests per instance"""
        if self.current_instances == 0:
            return 100
        return (request_count / self.current_instances) * 10  # Simplified model
    
    def scale(self, request_count):
        """Automatically scale based on demand"""
        cpu_load = self.get_cpu_utilization(request_count)
        
        if cpu_load > self.target_cpu and self.current_instances < self.max_instances:
            self.current_instances += 1
            print(f"SCALING UP: Added instance. Total: {self.current_instances}")
        elif cpu_load < self.target_cpu * 0.5 and self.current_instances > self.min_instances:
            self.current_instances -= 1
            print(f"SCALING DOWN: Removed instance. Total: {self.current_instances}")
        
        return self.current_instances

# Simulate traffic patterns
autoscaler = AutoScaler(min_instances=2, max_instances=10, target_cpu_utilization=70)

print("=== Simulating Traffic Over 10 Hours ===\n")
traffic_patterns = [100, 200, 500, 1000, 1500, 2000, 1800, 1200, 600, 300]

for hour, requests in enumerate(traffic_patterns, 1):
    instances = autoscaler.scale(requests)
    cpu = autoscaler.get_cpu_utilization(requests)
    print(f"Hour {hour}: Requests={requests}, Instances={instances}, CPU Load={cpu:.1f}%")

print("\nBENEFIT DEMONSTRATED: Cloud auto-scaling automatically provisions/deprovisions resources based on demand, ensuring performance while optimizing costs")
examples.explanation: This simulation demonstrates the scalability benefit of cloud computing. The AutoScaler class automatically adjusts the number of instances based on CPU utilization. When traffic increases, it scales out (adds instances); when traffic decreases, it scales in (removes instances). This elastic scalability is a fundamental cloud computing benefit.

examples.title: Vendor Lock-In Demonstrator - Cloud Limitation Analysis
examples.description: Demonstrates the vendor lock-in limitation of cloud computing by showing how migrating between cloud providers requires significant code changes due to proprietary APIs.
examples.code:
# Vendor Lock-In Demonstrator - Cloud Limitation Analysis
# Limitation: Proprietary APIs create dependency and migration challenges

# Example 1: Cloud-specific storage APIs (vendor lock-in)
class CloudStorage:
    """Base class demonstrating vendor lock-in issue"""
    def upload_file(self, filename, data):
        raise NotImplementedError
    
    def download_file(self, filename):
        raise NotImplementedError

class AWSS3Storage(CloudStorage):
    """AWS-specific implementation - uses boto3"""
    def __init__(self, bucket_name):
        self.bucket = bucket_name
        # import boto3  # Would need: pip install boto3
    
    def upload_file(self, filename, data):
        # AWS S3 specific code
        return f"s3://{self.bucket}/{filename}"
    
    def download_file(self, filename):
        return f"Downloading from s3://{self.bucket}/{filename}"

class AzureBlobStorage(CloudStorage):
    """Azure-specific implementation - uses azure-storage-blob"""
    def __init__(self, container_name):
        self.container = container_name
        # from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient
    
    def upload_file(self, filename, data):
        # Azure Blob specific code
        return f"https://storage.blob.core.windows.net/{self.container}/{filename}"
    
    def download_file(self, filename):
        return f"Downloading from https://storage.blob.core.windows.net/{self.container}/{filename}"

# Example 2: Demonstrating migration complexity
def migrate_aws_to_azure(aws_code):
    """Shows code changes required for migration"""
    migration_changes = {
        "boto3": "azure-storage-blob",
        "S3": "Blob Storage",
        "Bucket": "Container",
        "s3://": "https://storage.blob.core.windows.net/",
        "put_object": "upload_blob",
        "get_object": "download_blob"
    }
    
    print("=== Vendor Lock-In Migration Analysis ===")
    print(f"Original AWS Code Lines: ~50-100")
    print(f"Code Changes Required: Significant refactoring")
    print(f"Testing Effort: Extensive regression testing needed")
    print(f"Downtime: Potential business interruption")
    return migration_changes

# Demonstrate usage
aws_storage = AWSS3Storage("my-bucket")
azure_storage = AzureBlobStorage("my-container")

print("AWS S3 Upload:", aws_storage.upload_file("data.txt", "content"))
print("Azure Blob Upload:", azure_storage.upload_file("data.txt", "content"))

migrate_aws_to_azure(aws_storage)

print("\nLIMITATION DEMONSTRATED: Each cloud provider uses proprietary APIs and services, creating vendor lock-in. Migrating between providers requires significant code changes, increasing switching costs and reducing flexibility.")
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates the vendor lock-in limitation of cloud computing. Each cloud provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) offers unique services with proprietary APIs. When organizations commit to one provider, migrating to another becomes expensive and time-consuming because code must be rewritten. The example shows how the same functionality requires different implementations for AWS S3 vs Azure Blob Storage.
